text.title: LB Session Persistence Failure Modes
text.summary: 3 antipatterns observed when sticky sessions are configured. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: One backend in the pool always shows 4× the load of peers. Investigation: a single corporate customer's 5000 employees all share one NAT egress IP.
antipattern.root-cause: `ip_hash` (or `balance source`) used for sticky sessions; collapses NAT users onto one backend.
antipattern.fix: Switch to cookie-based persistence (`cookie SRVID insert indirect nocache` in HAProxy; app-managed cookie or Nginx Plus sticky cookie). Verify load evens out within a check interval.

antipattern.symptom: App was migrated to Redis-backed sessions a year ago, but the LB config still has `cookie SRVID ... maxconn 100`. Deploys are slow because operators "must drain stickies".
antipattern.root-cause: Stickiness config was never removed after externalising session storage.
antipattern.fix: Remove sticky directives. Pool returns to even distribution + deploys become straightforward rolling restarts. Confirm app sessions still work via end-to-end test.

antipattern.symptom: A backend was rolled out of the pool 3 days ago but is still receiving stray traffic from a small set of clients; rolling deploys never seem to "finish".
antipattern.root-cause: Sticky cookie has no TTL; entries persist until the LB process restarts.
antipattern.fix: Add `expire 1h` to stick-table or `cookie SRVID insert indirect nocache maxidle 3600 maxlife 86400`. Confirm stickiness expires on schedule by replaying logs.
